title: SEGMENT 4 — Brentuximab vedotin — Adcetris and the CD30 story

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Brentuximab vedotin — Adcetris and the CD30 story
The next major ADC, the one that really vindicated the concept after the Mylotarg mess, was brentuximab vedotin, marketed as Adcetris.

Approved in 2011 by the FDA, it was the first ADC approval in over a decade — Mylotarg had been withdrawn just the year before — and it landed in a clinical space where the medical need was screaming.
Hodgkin lymphoma and a rare related cancer called anaplastic large-cell lymphoma.
Patients who had failed chemotherapy and stem cell transplant.
People with no good options.
The target was CD30, a surface protein that sits very specifically on Hodgkin lymphoma cells — particularly the Reed-Sternberg cells that are pathognomonic for classical Hodgkin disease.
CD30 is essentially absent on most healthy tissues, except for a small population of activated lymphocytes.
So as targets go, this was about as clean as it gets.
The antibody was a chimeric anti-CD30 monoclonal called brentuximab.
So far, so unsurprising.
The interesting move was the payload.
Brentuximab vedotin uses monomethyl auristatin E, abbreviated MMAE.
Auristatins are a synthetic class derived from a natural product called dolastatin, which was originally isolated from a sea slug in the Indian Ocean — yes, really, a sea slug.
The structure was simplified and modified to make it more druggable, and MMAE was the result.
MMAE works by binding to tubulin — the protein that polymerizes to form microtubules — and preventing the assembly of the mitotic spindle.
Cells that try to divide can't separate their chromosomes; they arrest in mitosis and die.
MMAE is, in the language of pharmacology, somewhere in the picomolar to low nanomolar range of cytotoxicity.
You cannot give it to a patient as a free drug.
The therapeutic index is too narrow; the patient would die.
But hung off an antibody, delivered selectively to CD30-expressing cells, it becomes therapeutically useful.
This is the ADC promise in action.
The linker was another innovation that distinguished Adcetris from Mylotarg.
Instead of a hydrazone — the acid-labile chemistry that had caused so many problems with Mylotarg — the Adcetris team used a peptide linker called valine-citrulline, abbreviated vc.
Valine-citrulline is a two-amino-acid sequence that is recognized by a specific lysosomal enzyme called cathepsin B.

The linker is essentially invisible to enzymes in the bloodstream — the plasma proteases can't cut it efficiently — but inside the lysosome, where cathepsin B is abundant and active, the linker is cleaved cleanly, releasing the MMAE payload.
This is much more controlled than acid-labile chemistry.
The drug stays intact in circulation, and the payload is released only after internalization.
So Adcetris combined a clean target, a potent payload, and a well-designed enzymatically cleavable linker.
The clinical results reflected that.
In relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma after stem cell transplant, the overall response rate was about seventy-five percent, with about a third of patients achieving a complete response.
These were patients with essentially no other treatment options.
The drug was a clear win, and the FDA approved it on the strength of single-arm data.
In the years since, Adcetris has expanded into additional indications — frontline Hodgkin lymphoma in combination with chemotherapy, peripheral T-cell lymphoma, and others.
It now generates over a billion dollars a year in revenue and has become a backbone therapy for CD30-positive lymphomas.
But the bigger contribution of Adcetris was not just the drug itself.
It was the validation of an entire technology stack.

The MMAE payload, the valine-citrulline linker, the conjugation chemistry — all of this work was done by a company called Seattle Genetics, later renamed Seagen, and that platform became the template for an entire generation of ADCs.
Polatuzumab vedotin, enfortumab vedotin, tisotumab vedotin — you can hear the family resemblance in the names.
They all use MMAE, they all use valine-citrulline linkers, they all use the same conjugation chemistry.
Adcetris proved the technology stack worked, and then dozens of follow-on drugs used the same stack with different antibodies.
Seagen was eventually acquired by Pfizer in 2023 for forty-three billion dollars, which is a useful number to bookmark when you think about how the industry values ADC platforms.
Forty-three billion for the company that figured out how to make MMAE-vc ADCs reliably.
The chemistry of the linker and the chemistry of the payload, both pioneered for Adcetris, are now responsible for a substantial fraction of the global ADC market.
So Adcetris is the drug that did two things.
It rescued the ADC field from the post-Mylotarg slump, and it established a platform that other drugs would build on for the next fifteen years.
The next major ADC, T-DM1, would take a different approach — different payload, different linker, different conjugation — and prove that there was more than one way to skin this particular cat.
